title: Chapter 18: Cold Eternity
paragraphs: Night at the walls of Agdash descended not like a blessed shadow granting rest, but like a heavy, frozen shroud. Ash continued to fall, covering the meager camp of the four outcasts. They settled in a niche between two colossal buttresses of pale jade that protected them from the biting wasteland wind.

Yashil sat by a tiny, barely smoldering campfire made from fragments of old arrow shafts and dried moss. He silently moistened a piece of clean cloth with water from the elven flask and applied it to his cut cheekbone. The wound pulsed with a dull pain, but the goblin's heart was at peace.
Rene huddled in her heavy cloak, gracefully crossing her legs in torn stockings. The tip of her minty cigarette was the only bright spot in the gray dusk.

"We sit under the walls of a city that hates us and wait for the end of the world," the fox drawled melancholically, releasing a thin stream of smoke into the frosty air. "If someone had told me a century ago that I would end my days in the company of a pacifist, a crying demon, and a philosopher-wolf, I would have clawed his eyes out for lack of literary taste."
Dzhanavar, lying on his stomach at the very edge of the light from the fire, opened one amber eye. The fur on his mighty back was covered with a thin layer of frost.

"Do you know, little fox, what they say in the forest about the cold?" the werewolf yawned lazily, baring his fangs. "A wolf does not freeze. A wolf simply becomes more cold-blooded. Augh. And anyway, if you have an extra drag, I wouldn't refuse. My inner predator requires nicotine for reflections on the vanity of existence."
Rene only snorted contemptuously, adjusting the cigarette holder in her grip.
Kirah sat a little apart, hugging his massive knees with his arms. The demon looked into the darkness. His crimson veins glowed dimly, almost peacefully.
"Something is out there," the giant suddenly rumbled, and his voice trembled with a primal, unnatural cold. "Darkness, but... not like Zakhan's. It doesn't burn. It sucks the life out."

The campfire was instantly extinguished, as if someone had covered it with an invisible bell-jar. The air temperature dropped so sharply that the outcasts' breath turned into thick clouds of white steam. The ash falling from the sky froze in mid-air, turning into small crystals of ice.

From the impenetrable gloom beyond the boundary of the buttresses came footsteps. They were absolutely soundless, but each of those present felt them like blows of an icy hammer on an anvil. It smelled of old, centuries-old blood, a frosty night, and withered roses.

In the pale light of the runes smoldering on the wall of Agdash, a tall figure appeared. A man encased in flawless, somber black velvet of a vintage cut. The collar of his cloak propped up an aristocratically pale, sculpted face, untouched by a single emotion. His eyes were the color of dark ruby, and his black hair was impeccably styled.
Dzhanavar rose to his paws with a muffled snarl, reaching for his axe. Rene froze, her cigarette stopped a millimeter from her lips. The fox recognized this smell. The smell of high undead.

"Lower your iron, werewolf," the stranger said. His voice was soft, insinuating, and hauntingly beautiful, but from it wafted a centuries-old, absolute indifference. "If I had come for your lives, you would already be part of my retinue."
The man stopped a few paces from Yashil.

"I am Lord Valtor," the vampire introduced himself, and a light, predatory half-smile flickered on his pale lips. "The one who holds the Northern Rift of this city, raising fallen defenders so they may fight again, while the High Elves hide in their marble halls and discourse on the purity of blood."
Valtor turned his ruby eyes to the little goblin.

"Word reached my catacombs that a certain green-skinned creature this morning broke the will of an elven captain. Not with magic. And not with steel. But by offering his neck to the blade," the vampire slowly tilted his head, studying Yashil like a curious insect under glass. "The High Elves for centuries built pride into an absolute. But when the skies began to rot, their pride turned into fear. And you... you sit in the dirt, bleeding, and offer them your life. For what? Do you seek a beautiful death, goblin?"
Yashil calmly met the gaze of the immortal predator. In the vampire's eyes he saw neither hatred nor madness. Only the infinite, icy fatigue of a being who had survived all too many bygone eras.
"There is no such thing as a beautiful death, Lord Valtor," Yashil replied quietly. "There is only a death that has meaning. And a death that is devoid of it."

"Meaning?" Valtor let out a quiet, dry chuckle, devoid of all mirth. "The Void sooner or later will consume this world. The stones of Agdash will crumble, the forests of your werewolf will rot, and even my immortal legions will turn to dust. You bought the captain of the guard a couple of days of life at the cost of your wounded cheek. Но the Void doesn't care about your noble gestures. Your sacrifice is simply a deferred form of suicide."
Yashil slowly rose. He was tiny against the background of the tall, stately vampire, but in his posture was not a trace of subservience.
"I did not do it to stop the Void, Lord Valtor. The skies that tore our life apart, I cannot heal."
"Then why?"

"To stop the captain. So he himself would not become this Void," Yashil looked at the frozen ash at his feet. "You are right. The world is dying. We all, most likely, are doomed. Но if the end is inevitable, then the only thing that has true value is how we meet this end. We can turn into monsters, gnawing at each other's throats for an extra day of life. Or we can remain those who share the last sip of water."
Valtor was silent for a long time. Not a single muscle moved on his sculpted face.

"I have lived thousands of years, Yashil of the Rotten Hollow," the vampire said coldly. "I have seen empires born and die. Compassion is a defect. It is a crack in the armor through which your will flows out. Your empathy will burn you out from the inside. Look at your demon. He is woven of the Abyss, yet he cries from the pain of the world, and this makes him a pitiful, broken weapon."
Kirah growled hollowly behind Yashil's back, but not from aggression, rather because the vampire had mercilessly struck at his sorest spot.
But the goblin did not look away.

"If your heart is just a cold stone, Lord Valtor, then it, of course, will never burn or break. But it will not warm anyone in this darkness either. You take pride in your immortality. You do not age and do not feel pain. But tell me... if you have nothing to die for, and no one to cry for—are you even alive?"
The icy wind howling between the buttresses seemed to freeze.

Dzhanavar opened his maw, forgetting to close it. Rene looked at the little green philosopher with unfeigned, sincere admiration. To challenge an immortal vampire lord, armed only with words—that was an art available to few.

Valtor's eyes, the color of clotted blood, narrowed. A dangerous spark flickered in them. For a second it seemed he would simply tear the daring goblin to pieces. But then the tension faded. The vampire regained his flawless, cold composure.

"You are a striking paradox, goblin," Valtor's voice became even quieter, almost dropping to a whisper. "Your words are naive, like a child's, but there is a poison in them that gets stuck in one's thoughts. You call me a dead man because I have forgotten how to feel. Perhaps you are right. Or perhaps you just haven't seen the true face of despair yet."
The Lord slowly turned, the hem of his black velvet cloak flared, raising a cloud of icy dust.
"The High Elves will not let you in. They will watch you freeze here, under their ideal walls. Soon the skies will truly tear apart. And then I will see how long your naive light lasts, Yashil."
The vampire took a step into the darkness and began to dissolve in the frosty mist. But before disappearing completely, his voice reached the outcasts for the last time:

"But I confess... it's been a long time since I've been so curious to observe someone else's fate. Keep this fire until the end, goblin. Perhaps when the time comes, I will come to look at it once more."
Night again came into its own. The ice on the ash melted, and Dzhanavar, exhaling noisily, reached for the fragments of branches to start the fire anew.

"Augh..." the werewolf said with feeling, wiping sweat from his brow. "What a character. More pathos than me, and that, let me tell you, is a serious claim. But you got him, brother. Smashed him verbally against the marble."

Rene silently handed her cigarette to Yashil. He softly, with a smile, shook his head, refusing. The fox shrugged and took a deep drag. "The seed is cast into dead soil, Yashil," she said quietly. "Let's see if anything but thistles can grow from it."

And somewhere far in the wastelands, beyond the visibility of Agdash, through the ash and darkness trudged a lonely figure in heavy, black plate armor. His flame was fading, his soul was torn to pieces, and he did not know that his path led to this very fire.
